Name: gpu_kernel_optimization_via_dynamic_memory_access
Title: Dynamic Memory Access Patterns for Optimized GPU Kernels in LLM Inference
Short Hypothesis: Dynamic memory access patterns can significantly enhance the performance of GPU kernels for large language model (LLM) inference by reducing memory stalls and improving data locality without requiring extensive manual tuning.
Related Work: Existing work, such as TritonForge and Astra, focuses on automated kernel optimization and GPU performance. However, they often rely on static optimization strategies that do not adapt to dynamic workloads. This proposal distinguishes itself by implementing a dynamic memory access strategy that adjusts in real-time based on workload characteristics, enhancing efficiency without the overhead of extensive manual tuning.
Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) demand high-performance GPU kernels for efficient inference, yet existing optimization techniques often fall short in addressing dynamic memory access patterns that can lead to significant performance bottlenecks. This proposal introduces a novel framework that dynamically adjusts memory access patterns in GPU kernels during inference based on real-time profiling data. By leveraging lightweight runtime analysis, we will identify and adapt kernel memory access strategies to enhance data locality and minimize memory stalls. Our approach will be validated through a series of empirical tests on standard LLM benchmarks, measuring wall-clock latency, throughput, and energy efficiency. We expect to demonstrate that our dynamic memory access framework can achieve substantial speedups over static optimization techniques, providing a new direction for GPU kernel performance enhancement in LLM applications.
Experiments: Implement a baseline GPU kernel for LLM inference using static memory access patterns.
Develop a dynamic memory access framework that adjusts memory access strategies based on runtime profiling data.
Conduct A/B testing, comparing the dynamic access pattern kernel against the baseline kernel under various input workloads.
Measure performance metrics such as wall-clock latency, throughput (tokens-per-second), and energy consumption per token.
Collect detailed profiler traces to analyze occupancy, memory access patterns, and stall reasons.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential overhead from runtime profiling may affect performance gains.
The dynamic adjustments may not generalize well across all types of workloads.
Limited by the hardware capabilities of the GPU used for testing, which may not exhibit the full potential of the dynamic strategy.
